source_title: Best Of 2020: Part Three: The Rich Roll Podcast

paragraphs.speaker: rich
paragraphs.name: Rich Roll
paragraphs.text: That's Matthew McConaughey, and this is part three of our very special best of 2020 edition of the Rich Roll Podcast. Happy New Year's Eve, everybody, or New Year's Day, whenever you find yourself tuning into this. That is one of the amazing things about sobriety, waking up fresh and clear on New Year's Day. I count 23 consecutive New Year's Days without a hangover, which I gotta say feels pretty awesome. If you're rethinking your relationship to alcohol or drugs, you don't have to use or drink again if you don't want to, ever. If I could do it, I know for a fact that you can too.

paragraphs.speaker: matthew
paragraphs.name: Matthew McConaughey
paragraphs.text: Each one of us need to look in the mirror. How can I be a little bit better? What can I do a little bit better? I want to be great. I want to be perfect. There is no best. How can I be a little bit better? How can I be a little more fair? How can I understand that my brother and sister are also hurting maybe in more different ways than I am? How can I have a conversation without a condemnation? How can I have more patience to take a breath and listen and let someone who hasn't been heard speak more loudly than maybe they need to, but hear them?

paragraphs.speaker: erin
paragraphs.name: Erin Brockovich
paragraphs.text: Curiosity. I was smart enough to read the medical reports. So it didn't take a rocket scientist to figure out what the hell, a lot of T-cells. What are those? I looked it up. So white counts. What does that mean? Looked it up. All of this stuff was off the chart and mom kicked in and curiosity kicked in and I'm like, what if that was my child? I'd be worried. And that's what kicked me off was just my own. Oh my gosh, this is weird. I'm curious. Why would this be happening? What's wrong with the children? What would I do if I saw blood work like this on my kid?

paragraphs.speaker: erin
paragraphs.name: Erin Brockovich
paragraphs.text: We, the people. I think we, the people, have forgotten to believe in we, the people. We are water. We are sustained by water. Do not think that we might not have water, especially if we keep going at this rate. And let's get serious about climate change. We talk about Johannesburg, South Africa, where they literally were going to have day zero. But here's what they did, what we're not doing, and where we can learn from. The people responded, the people were involved, the people rationed, they did what they had to do, the agencies responded, they became prepared, and they diverted day zero.

paragraphs.speaker: matthew
paragraphs.name: Matthew McConaughey
paragraphs.text: How do we make this time not just a flash in the pan? How do we be honest where the choices we make for ourselves selfishly are also the best choices for the most amount of people? I'd say this man, start off with trying to create more green lights for yourself and others and see where those two meet and see that actually being selfless is actually a very selfish act. Creating more for others is actually very selfish for yourself as well. And try to make sure your selfish choices for yourself also light the way for more people as well.

paragraphs.speaker: shane
paragraphs.name: Shane Parrish
paragraphs.text: The decisions where you know the outcome or you can't fail are not really worth thinking about. You have to determine if failure is something you can handle or not and often we are going to fail and how do we recover from that? Is super important to achieving success because the odds that you succeed the first time you try something are pretty slim. Often life is just getting up one more time than you fall down. Going back to decision making again reversible irreversible like where can you fail? Where can you fail and how we think about what failure means? I think we attach so much meaning to it and there's so much ego that is infused into that.

paragraphs.speaker: chris_m
paragraphs.name: Chris Mosier
paragraphs.text: Transgender women and girls have been competing with women and girls for years without problem with their peers. The real problem is the lack of access, the lack of resources, the pay inequity that exists within women's sports because of systemic discrimination. I think that sport is one of the most magical places for people to exist. Sport is a vehicle for social change and the change that happens within sports in terms of policies and acceptance and inclusion can really be a guide for the rest of the world.
